response:
Decision recorded as `docs/decisions/0045-sync-before-spend-mechanized-claim-deferred.md` (5,869/6,000 chars, manually verified against `adr-lint.mjs`'s rules since `node` execution wasn't approved in this session).

**Decision:** Mechanize the sync-before-spend check as a script a dispatched session runs immediately before spending materially and again before the final push — not another CLAUDE.md prose line. **Defer the claim protocol.**

**Why:** The incident (`ISSUE-106.md:6`) was a *stale read* (PR #97 merged while the session's clone was fresh-at-start, stale-by-use) — a re-sync directly catches that mechanism, cheaply, without new infra. Prose-only repeats a failure this repo already named its own precedent for: ADR 0042's cost-pilot rule was prose-first and its revisit trigger states prose discipline recurs until mechanized. The claim protocol is withheld because its known permission gap (`ISSUE-106.md:15`) makes it *silently* unusable in exactly the dispatches where it'd matter most — a mechanism that fails invisibly is worse than none.

**Load-bearing assumption (stated, not just an acceptance-criterion aside):** real collisions in this repo's dispatch pattern predominantly take the stale-read form the sync script catches, rather than a true concurrent-dispatch race where neither session has landed work before the other starts spending.

**Failure class accepted:** exactly that concurrent-dispatch race — two sessions dispatched close enough together that a sync check finds nothing stale to catch. This is named as the revisit trigger: if it recurs, the claim protocol ships, designed as a real lock (not a bare comment, per the premortem's TOCTOU point).

Options B (claim alone) and C (both) were rejected as either unreliable (permission gap) or gold-plating a not-yet-observed failure mode; D (do nothing) was rejected because the issue itself calls the incident's self-correction "luck, not design."

Implementing the actual script and wiring the CLAUDE.md forcing-functions pointer is follow-up work (would normally get a GitHub issue per ADR 0021, but this sandbox has no configured remote to file one against) — flagging that as the next step rather than doing it silently.

id: 0045
title: "Sync-before-spend mechanizes as a script; claim protocol deferred"
status: accepted
summary: "Duplicate-spend prevention (#106, #30 executed twice) mechanizes as a re-sync script run before spending and again before final push, replacing prose-only recall; the claim protocol is deferred (permission gap makes it a silent no-op, and even where usable it only narrows a TOCTOU race), revisited if a true concurrent-dispatch collision (not a stale read) recurs."
---

# 0045 — Sync-before-spend mechanizes as a script; claim protocol deferred

- Date: 2026-07-14
- Owner: PM
- Panel: lean-process-engineer (poka-yoke vs gold-plate), process-economist (build cost vs
  collision cost), session-operator (does the check survive a live dispatched session).
- Context: ISSUE-106.md:6 — a dispatched session spent ~$5-8 executing #30 in full while PR #97
  had already merged and closed it concurrently; the clone was "fresh-at-start but stale-by-use."
  ISSUE-106.md:10 — nothing forces a re-sync immediately before spend or a visible claim. Open
  question: is a prose CLAUDE.md rule enough, or does this need mechanizing.

## Decision
Mechanize the sync check: a script (`git fetch origin main` + re-read the target issue + search
PRs referencing it) that a dispatched session runs immediately before spending materially on an
issue, and again before the final push — not a CLAUDE.md prose reminder alone. Houses under
CLAUDE.md's Forcing functions bullet (the existing pattern: adr-lint, check-workflow.mjs,
`/retrospect`), pointing at the script rather than restating the check. The **claim protocol is
NOT adopted now** — deferred. House alongside it, per ISSUE-106.md:18: dispatch prompts must
mandate foreground waits on long child runs (a detached-background subagent stalled twice
elsewhere).

## Justification
Cost x risk x value: the incident's mechanism was a stale read (ISSUE-106.md:6), which a re-sync
immediately before spend addresses directly, at near-zero build cost (a command sequence, not new
infra) — an easily-skipped mental step becomes a deterministic, model-runnable check. Prose-only
(the issue's literal Option A) repeats a failure this repo has already named: ADR 0042's cost-pilot
rule was prose-first, and its own revisit trigger (0042:38) is "recurs past a decidable gate ->
needs a hard stop" — prose discipline recurs until mechanized here, so another prose rule bets
against a trigger already pulled once. The claim protocol's marginal benefit is narrower than it
looks: it only helps a sub-case not yet observed (two sessions dispatched close enough together
that neither's work lands before the other starts), while its cost is concrete now — ISSUE-106.md:15
names dispatches that withhold issue-write permission, so a claim can silently fail to post, and a
second session then reads "unclaimed" for the same reason the first read "open." A mechanism that
fails invisibly under a condition already known to occur is worse than none (false confidence), so
it waits until the gap it fills is demonstrated, not hypothetical.

## Assumptions
- [verified] the incident's mechanism was a stale read, not a same-time race between two spending
  sessions: PR #97 merged and closed #30 while the dispatched session's clone was
  "fresh-at-start but stale-by-use" (ISSUE-106.md:6) — a sync-before-spend re-check reads this
  directly and would have caught it.
- [verified] the claim protocol's permission gap is real, not hypothetical: ISSUE-106.md:15 states
  dispatch instructions "sometimes withhold" issue-write permission ("don't change the issues").
- [checkable-doc] the Forcing-functions bullet is the right home for the script pointer, per
  CLAUDE.md's existing pattern of naming adr-lint / check-workflow.mjs / `/retrospect` as the
  enforcement layer rather than restating checks in prose — verified against CLAUDE.md's Muda
  section, 2026-07-14.
- [unverifiable] real collisions in this repo's dispatch pattern predominantly take the stale-read
  form (catchable by re-sync) rather than a true concurrent-dispatch race (two sessions overlapping
  before either lands work) — REOPEN-IF: a collision recurs between two sessions dispatched close
  enough together that neither had merged before the other started spending, so a sync check could
  not have caught it. That signal adds the claim protocol (or a real lock, since ISSUE-106.md's
  premortem notes a non-atomic claim/comment is itself only a narrowed race, not a mutex).

## Rejected alternatives
- Sync rule as prose only (issue's literal Option A) — the issue's own text flags "same enforcement
  weakness as any prose rule"; ADR 0042 is house precedent that prose discipline recurs until
  mechanized.
- Claim protocol alone (Option B) — permission-gated dispatches make it silently unusable exactly
  when needed, and it doesn't address the incident's actual (stale-read) mechanism.
- Both, sync + claim (Option C) — front-loads a second mechanism, carrying B's weaknesses, for a
  failure mode (concurrent-dispatch race) not yet observed; gold-plate against this repo's own
  "prevent > detect... don't gold-plate" rule until the REOPEN-IF fires.
- Do nothing (Option D) — ISSUE-106.md:6 calls the redundant run's self-correction "luck, not
  design"; recurring ~$5-8 duplicate spend isn't tolerable policy on one lucky miss.

## Revisit triggers
- A collision recurs that a sync-before-spend re-check could not have caught (two sessions
  dispatched close together, neither merged before the other started) -> adopt the claim protocol,
  designed as a real lock (atomic check-and-claim), not a bare comment/label.
- The sync script itself gets skipped or misused in a live dispatched session -> scripting alone
  didn't close the enforcement gap; escalate to whatever wires session dispatch, if that surface
  exists.
